title: CDMGNotation

% Definition file for row def_3_2 -- "CDMGNotation".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker dispatched by it) fills the body below with the
% Lean-faithful definition statement(s). Stays close to the lecture notes.
%
% This file is a sibling of `main.tex` inside `<subsection>/tex/`. The
% `subfiles` package lets it compile both standalone (resolving its
% preamble via `main.tex`) and as part of `main.tex` via `\subfile{...}`.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: def_3_2
% title: CDMGNotation
\def\rowref{def\_3\_2}
\phantomsection\label{def_3_2}

\begin{Not}[CDMGNotation]
    \label{not-cdmg}
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}).
    We introduce the following seven shorthand notations, each defined as a literal abbreviation for a set-theoretic statement over the four components $J, V, E, L$ of $G$.
    The visual macros $\tuh, \hut, \huh, \suh, \hus, \sus$ are notation only; the load-bearing definitional content is the right-hand side of each abbreviation below.
    \begin{enumerate}
        %\item $G(V|\doit(J))$ to represent the graph $(J,V,E,L)$, where $E$ and $L$ are kept implicit in this notation.
        \item $v \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; v \in J \cup V$.
        \item $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; (v_1, v_2) \in E$.
        \item $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; (v_2, v_1) \in E$.
        \item $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; (v_1, v_2) \in L$.
              Because $L$ is required to be symmetric by the CDMG definition (def \ref{def-cdmg}: $(v_1, v_2) \in L \Longleftrightarrow (v_2, v_1) \in L$ for all $v_1, v_2$), this abbreviation is itself symmetric in its two arguments:
              \[ v_1 \huh v_2 \in G \;\Longleftrightarrow\; v_2 \huh v_1 \in G. \]
        \item $v_1 \suh v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; \bigl(v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(v_1 \huh v_2 \in G\bigr)$, equivalently $(v_1, v_2) \in E \,\lor\, (v_1, v_2) \in L$.
              Because the $\huh$-disjunct is symmetric in its arguments (item~4), swapping $v_1 \leftrightarrow v_2$ in this disjunction turns $\suh$ into $\hus$:
              \[ v_1 \suh v_2 \in G \;\Longleftrightarrow\; v_2 \hus v_1 \in G. \]
        \item $v_1 \hus v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; \bigl(v_1 \hut v_2 \in G\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(v_1 \huh v_2 \in G\bigr)$, equivalently $(v_2, v_1) \in E \,\lor\, (v_1, v_2) \in L$.
              By the same $\huh$-symmetry of item~4, $\hus$ becomes $\suh$ under swap of its arguments:
              \[ v_1 \hus v_2 \in G \;\Longleftrightarrow\; v_2 \suh v_1 \in G. \]
        \item $v_1 \sus v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; \bigl(v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(v_1 \hut v_2 \in G\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(v_1 \huh v_2 \in G\bigr)$, equivalently $(v_1, v_2) \in E \,\lor\, (v_2, v_1) \in E \,\lor\, (v_1, v_2) \in L$.

              \emph{Exhaustiveness of the three disjuncts.}
              These three configurations enumerate every edge configuration a CDMG admits between an ordered pair of nodes $(v_1, v_2)$.
              Although mechanically reading the placeholder convention ``$\star$ stands for arrowhead or tail'' would suggest a fourth, tail-tail case ``$v_1 \tut v_2$'', no edge type of a CDMG carries tails on both ends: per def \ref{def-cdmg}, directed edges in $E$ have one tail and one head, and bidirected edges in $L$ have two heads, so no tail-tail edge type exists.
              The case $v_1 \tut v_2$ is therefore vacuous as a CDMG edge and is excluded by definition, not omitted by oversight.
              Any case analysis on ``$v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$'' may assume exhaustiveness over exactly the three listed alternatives $\{\tuh, \hut, \huh\}$.

              \emph{Symmetry of $\sus$.}
              Under the swap $v_1 \leftrightarrow v_2$, the three disjuncts pair off: $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ exchanges with $v_2 \hut v_1 \in G$ (items~2~$\leftrightarrow$~3), and $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$ exchanges with $v_2 \huh v_1 \in G$ by the $\huh$-symmetry of item~4. Hence $\sus$ is symmetric in its two arguments:
              \[ v_1 \sus v_2 \in G \;\Longleftrightarrow\; v_2 \sus v_1 \in G. \]
    \end{enumerate}
    A ``$\star$'' appearing in the diagrammatic shorthands ($\suh$, $\hus$, $\sus$) is a placeholder reading ``arrowhead or tail''; in $\sus$ the would-be tail-tail combination is vacuous as a CDMG edge type (see item~7), so $\sus$ ranges over exactly the three configurations enumerated there.
\end{Not}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}). We introduce the following seven shorthand notations, each defined as a literal abbreviation for a set-theoretic statement over the four components $J, V, E, L$ of $G$. The visual macros $\tuh, \hut, \huh, \suh, \hus, \sus$ are notation only; the load-bearing definitional content is the right-hand side of each abbreviation below.

  1. $v \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; v \in J \cup V$.
  2. $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; (v_1, v_2) \in E$.
  3. $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; (v_2, v_1) \in E$.
  4. $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; (v_1, v_2) \in L$. Because $L$ is required to be symmetric by the CDMG definition (def \ref{def-cdmg}: $(v_1, v_2) \in L \Longleftrightarrow (v_2, v_1) \in L$ for all $v_1, v_2$), this abbreviation is itself symmetric in its two arguments: \[ v_1 \huh v_2 \in G \;\Longleftrightarrow\; v_2 \huh v_1 \in G. \]
  5. $v_1 \suh v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; \bigl(v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(v_1 \huh v_2 \in G\bigr)$, equivalently $(v_1, v_2) \in E \,\lor\, (v_1, v_2) \in L$. Because the $\huh$-disjunct is symmetric in its arguments (item 4), swapping $v_1 \leftrightarrow v_2$ in this disjunction turns $\suh$ into $\hus$: \[ v_1 \suh v_2 \in G \;\Longleftrightarrow\; v_2 \hus v_1 \in G. \]
  6. $v_1 \hus v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; \bigl(v_1 \hut v_2 \in G\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(v_1 \huh v_2 \in G\bigr)$, equivalently $(v_2, v_1) \in E \,\lor\, (v_1, v_2) \in L$. By the same $\huh$-symmetry of item 4, $\hus$ becomes $\suh$ under swap of its arguments: \[ v_1 \hus v_2 \in G \;\Longleftrightarrow\; v_2 \suh v_1 \in G. \]
  7. $v_1 \sus v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; \bigl(v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(v_1 \hut v_2 \in G\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(v_1 \huh v_2 \in G\bigr)$, equivalently $(v_1, v_2) \in E \,\lor\, (v_2, v_1) \in E \,\lor\, (v_1, v_2) \in L$.

    Exhaustiveness of the three disjuncts. These three configurations enumerate every edge configuration a CDMG admits between an ordered pair of nodes $(v_1, v_2)$. Although mechanically reading the placeholder convention “$\star$ stands for arrowhead or tail” would suggest a fourth, tail-tail case “$v_1 \tut v_2$”, no edge type of a CDMG carries tails on both ends: per def \ref{def-cdmg}, directed edges in $E$ have one tail and one head, and bidirected edges in $L$ have two heads, so no tail-tail edge type exists. The case $v_1 \tut v_2$ is therefore vacuous as a CDMG edge and is excluded by definition, not omitted by oversight. Any case analysis on “$v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$” may assume exhaustiveness over exactly the three listed alternatives $\{\tuh, \hut, \huh\}$.

    Symmetry of $\sus$. Under the swap $v_1 \leftrightarrow v_2$, the three disjuncts pair off: $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ exchanges with $v_2 \hut v_1 \in G$ (items 2 $\leftrightarrow$ 3), and $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$ exchanges with $v_2 \huh v_1 \in G$ by the $\huh$-symmetry of item 4. Hence $\sus$ is symmetric in its two arguments: \[ v_1 \sus v_2 \in G \;\Longleftrightarrow\; v_2 \sus v_1 \in G. \]

A “$\star$” appearing in the diagrammatic shorthands ($\suh$, $\hus$, $\sus$) is a placeholder reading “arrowhead or tail”; in $\sus$ the would-be tail-tail combination is vacuous as a CDMG edge type (see item 7), so $\sus$ ranges over exactly the three configurations enumerated there.

lean_blocks.code:
instance instMembership : Membership Node (CDMG Node) where
  mem G v := v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V
def tuh (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := (v1, v2) ∈ G.E
def hut (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := (v2, v1) ∈ G.E
def huh (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := (v1, v2) ∈ G.L

def suh (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop :=
  G.tuh v1 v2 ∨ G.huh v1 v2

def hus (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop :=
  G.hut v1 v2 ∨ G.huh v1 v2

def sus (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop :=
  G.tuh v1 v2 ∨ G.hut v1 v2 ∨ G.huh v1 v2

addition_to_the_LN:
[sus_omits_tail_tail_despite_star_eq_head_or_tail] CDMGs do not admit a tail-tail edge type; consequently the placeholder rule "the star stands for arrowhead or tail" yields only the three enumerated configurations in item 7 (the tail-tail case "$v_1 \tut v_2$" is excluded by definition, not omitted by oversight). Any case analysis on "$v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$" may assume exhaustiveness over exactly the three listed alternatives $\{\tuh, \hut, \huh\}$.

[huh_visual_symmetry_vs_ordered_pair_in_L] The relation $L$ is required to be symmetric: for all $v_1, v_2$, $(v_1, v_2) \in L$ if and only if $(v_2, v_1) \in L$. Consequently, $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G \iff v_2 \huh v_1 \in G$, and the symmetry of the $\huh$-disjunct propagates to items 5–7 accordingly.
lean_explanation:
The naming of the six relations reads as a two-letter shape of an edge: `t` is a tail, `h` an arrowhead, `s` the LN's "star" placeholder for "either tail or head". So `tuh` is tail-on-the-left, head-on-the-right (the directed edge $v_1 \to v_2$), `hut` is its mirror ($v_1 \leftarrow v_2$), `huh` is head-on-both-ends (the bidirected $v_1 \leftrightarrow v_2$), and `suh`, `hus`, `sus` are the star-disjunctions that fan out into the two- or three-case `∨` listed in the LN. The middle `u` is just a connector, and the names are chosen to grep verbatim against the LaTeX macros `\tuh`, `\hut`, etc.

Item 1 is registered as a `Membership` typeclass instance rather than a plain definition. This is what makes the literal Lean text `v ∈ G` typecheck: Lean's `∈` desugars by looking up an instance `Membership α β` where `α` is the element type and `β` the container type. Providing one for `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` plugs the CDMG into the same `∈` notation that `Finset`, `Set`, etc. already use, with no clash — typeclass resolution dispatches on the container type. The body itself contains a second, inner `∈` on the `Finset` `G.J ∪ G.V`, which resolves to mathlib's own `Finset` membership instance.

The remaining six are plain `def`s returning `Prop`, not `Bool`. A `Prop`-valued definition is a logical proposition you prove or assume; a `Bool`-valued one would be a computation. Keeping them propositional lets case-splits use `rcases … with h | h` directly on the `∨`s. Each is declared inside `namespace CDMG`, so dot-notation `G.tuh v1 v2` works: Lean sees `G : CDMG Node` and rewrites it to `CDMG.tuh G v1 v2`, which lines up with the LN's left-to-right reading $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$.
design_choices:
**Encode `v ∈ G` as a `Membership` instance rather than a plain `def`.** The notes write `v \in G` literally at every use site, and a `def CDMG.Mem` would force every consumer to spell `G.Mem v` or `CDMG.Mem G v`, breaking the surface correspondence with the notes. Registering a `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance lets `v ∈ G` parse on the nose, coexisting cleanly with mathlib's `Membership α (Finset α)` because the second type argument differs (`Finset Node` vs `CDMG Node`), so type-class resolution dispatches each occurrence to the right instance without ascriptions.

**Keep six separate `Prop` predicates instead of a single `inductive EdgeType` with a `G.edgeOf : Node → Node → Option EdgeType` projection.** Collapsing the three primitive shapes into one constructor sum was rejected because every downstream consumer in chapter 3 - set-builder forms like $Pa^G(v) := \{w \mid w \tuh v \in G\}$, walk-edge predicates, and acyclic chain conditions $v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n$ - pattern-matches against individual edge symbols. An ADT would force a `match ... | EdgeType.tailHead => ...` split at every site, hide the symbol-per-relation grep correspondence, and add boilerplate the notes never write. Mirroring the notes' enumeration one-for-one is a one-shot cost paid here.

**Use dot-notation `G.tuh v1 v2` rather than infix `notation` macros like `v1 →[G] v2`.** Every infix would have to carry an explicit `[G]` annotation because downstream rows (hard intervention, node splitting) compare a graph with its rewritten copy, and the resulting `v1 →[G] v2` is visually heavier than `G.tuh v1 v2` while losing the exact grep-match to the notes' macro names.

**Prefer `def` over `abbrev` for every shorthand.** An `abbrev` would auto-unfold the body at every elaboration site, erasing the named abstraction the notes introduce; this matters most for `suh`, `hus`, and `sus`, where unfolding would explode every adjacency-driven proof into a two- or three-way disjunction. The `def` form leaves unfolding to explicit `unfold` or `simp only` invocations on demand.

**Define `hut` primitively as `(v2, v1) ∈ G.E`, not as `G.tuh v2 v1` or `G.tuh ∘ Prod.swap`.** A composition spelling would force consumers to chain `Function.comp_apply` and `Prod.swap_apply` rewrites before any `Finset` lemma can fire, paying that price at every walk and parent proof; the direct spelling lands on the literal `Finset` membership under a single `simp only [CDMG.hut]`.

**Treat the symmetry of `huh` as a corollary of `hL_symm`, not as part of the definition.** Inlining symmetry (e.g. `(v1, v2) ∈ G.L ∨ (v2, v1) ∈ G.L`) was rejected because it would make `huh` and the underlying $L$-membership lemmas drift apart, forcing every `hL_symm` invocation to rewrite through `huh` as well. Likewise, irreflexivity is left on the structure rather than baked in, preserving the distinction between data (membership in $L$) and axiom (no self-loops).

**Spell `sus` as the three-disjunct `tuh ∨ hut ∨ huh` in exactly that order, not four disjuncts and not a merged `(v1, v2) ∈ G.E ∪ G.L`.** The mechanical reading "star = arrowhead or tail" would suggest a fourth tail-tail case, but CDMGs admit no such edge type; the operator clarification makes the three-case analysis exhaustive by definition. A merged-union spelling was rejected because `E` and `L` are not disjoint as subsets of `Node × Node`, so the union would lose the two- or three-case decomposition that `rcases` relies on. The left-to-right order matches the notes verbatim, keeping case names aligned with the reading.
